channel.title: Paranormal on Alien Truth Social
channel.description: Recent content in Paranormal on Alien Truth Social

item.title: Sightings
item.description:
Sightings is an American paranormal and news television show that began in the 1990s. Initially airing as a special titled The UFO Report: Sightings in 1991, it evolved into a weekly program investigating topics like UFOs, ghosts, and Bigfoot. The show was presented in an investigative news format and hosted by Tim White. Sightings aired on various networks, including Fox, and later in syndication, before concluding its run in 1998 with special episodes.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sightings_%28TV_series%29

item.title: Sightings (TV series)
item.description:
Sightings was a paranormal-themed television series that aired from 1991 to 1997, featuring an investigative news format hosted by Tim White. The program covered a variety of fringe topics, including UFO sightings, ghosts, and Bigfoot, and was executive produced by Henry Winkler and Ann Daniel. Following its original run on Fox and in syndication, the show’s legacy continued through various specials and the 2002 television movie Sightings: Heartland Ghost. It remains a notable example of 1990s paranormal television that explored controversial subjects like animal mutilations and extraterrestrial encounters.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sightings_%28TV_series%29

item.title: They Knew Too Much About Flying Saucers
item.description:
They Knew Too Much About Flying Saucers is a 1956 book by Gray Barker that holds historical significance as the first text to introduce the “Men in Black” trope into UFO conspiracy culture. Barker, a member of the International Flying Saucer Bureau, alleged that researchers like Albert K. Bender were being intimidated and silenced by mysterious figures to prevent the disclosure of extraterrestrial truths. While the book became a bestseller and influenced decades of paranormal folklore, later investigations—including comments by collaborator John Sherwood—suggest that Barker significantly embellished or fabricated these accounts to capitalize on public interest. The work ultimately shaped the foundational imagery for modern pop culture franchises, including The X-Files and the Men in Black film series.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/They_Knew_Too_Much_About_Flying_Saucers

item.title: British UFO Research Association
item.description:
The British UFO Research Association (BUFORA) is a UK-based organization founded in 1964 to investigate reported UFO phenomena. The group conducts regular investigations and manages witness support services, reporting that approximately 95% of the cases they review are identified as hoaxes. Throughout its history, the association has faced controversy, including instances where it mistakenly validated fraudulent sightings and endorsed the 1995 film Alien Autopsy as authentic.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_UFO_Research_Association

item.title: Interdimensional UFO hypothesis
item.description:
The interdimensional UFO hypothesis (IUH) proposes that UFO sightings are not necessarily extraterrestrial in origin but result from encounters with entities or phenomena from coexisting, unseen dimensions. Notable ufologists such as Meade Layne, John Keel, J. Allen Hynek, and Jacques Vallée have advanced this theory, suggesting that these anomalies have appeared throughout history under various guises, including mythological or supernatural events. Proponents argue that the recurring patterns and characteristics of these sightings, which often defy conventional space-travel physics, are better explained by the existence of “interlocking universes” rather than planetary visitation.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdimensional_UFO_hypothesis

item.title: Mutilation of "Snippy" the horse
item.description:
The 1967 death of an Appaloosa mare named “Snippy” near Alamosa, Colorado, gained national attention after reports suggested the animal had been mysteriously mutilated by extraterrestrial entities. While sensationalized media accounts linked the incident to UFOs, later investigations by the Condon Committee and others attributed the death to natural causes and gunshot wounds inflicted by local individuals. Despite these findings, the case remains a foundational element of UFO folklore and the study of alleged animal mutilations. The horse’s skeletal remains were ultimately acquired by the UFO Watchtower in 2021 as a tourist attraction.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mutilation_of_%22Snippy%22_the_horse

item.title: The Unexplained Files
item.description:
The Unexplained Files is an American documentary television series that aired on Science Channel. The series investigated paranormal and unexplained phenomena across two seasons, with the first season having 8 episodes and the second season planning 12. While it was the most watched series on the Science Channel during its first season, critics noted its tendency to focus on sensationalized theories over scientific explanations. One review highlighted how a veterinarian’s explanation for cattle mutilations was given minimal airtime compared to “outlandish theories,” leading to criticism of the show as biased.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Unexplained_Files

item.title: Area 51: The Dreamland Chronicles
item.description:
Area 51: The Dreamland Chronicles is a 1997 non-fiction book by David Darlington about Area 51 in Nevada. Reviews noted the book’s service in detailing the site’s early history while also criticizing its inclusion of accounts from “alien seekers” and “saucer nuts.” The book has been called a definitive work on Area 51 and its cultural impact, with one review describing it as even-handed towards ufologists. It was published by Henry Holt & Company.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Area_51:_The_Dreamland_Chronicles

item.title: International UFO Congress
item.description:
The International UFO Congress is an annual conference established in 1991 in Arizona that focuses on ufology. It moved to the Phoenix, Arizona area in 2011 after previously being held in Laughlin, Nevada. The event features presentations on topics like alien abduction, UFO sightings, and paranormal experiences, with speakers from around the world. The conference has changed ownership multiple times since its inception.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_UFO_Congress

item.title: New Lands
item.description:
New Lands is the second nonfiction book by Charles Fort, published in 1923 by Boni & Liveright. The book primarily addresses astronomical anomalies and is described as a satirical critique of astronomers. Fort criticizes astronomical predictions and scientific dogma, expanding on his theories of a “Super-Sargasso Sea” and continents above Earth. Despite its content, New Lands is considered Fort’s worst-regarded book, with its speculations on sky continents and Earth’s shape being seen as dated. The book had poor sales, leading to Boni & Liveright declining to publish Fort’s subsequent work.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Lands

item.title: National Institute for Discovery Science (NIDSci)
item.description:
The National Institute for Discovery Science (NIDSci) was a privately financed research organization that operated from 1995 to 2004 in Las Vegas, Nevada. Founded by Robert Bigelow, its purpose was to research fringe science and paranormal topics, particularly ufology, though it also investigated anomalies beyond just UFOs. NIDSci conducted research into areas like cattle mutilation and black triangle reports, and it purchased Skinwalker Ranch. The organization established a hotline in 1999 to receive reports of unusual occurrences, receiving over 5,000 submissions.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Institute_for_Discovery_Science

item.title: Norio Fernando Hayakawa
item.description:
Norio Hayakawa is an American activist born in Yokohama, Japan, in 1944. He is known for his UFOlogy investigations in New Mexico and the American Southwest. Hayakawa has appeared on Coast to Coast AM and was involved in organizing the Area 51 People’s Rally. He also led a Nippon TV crew in 1990 to investigate paranormal activity in Dulce, New Mexico.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Norio_Hayakawa
